root_cause_candidates.cause: Symptom service (frontend) shows loss but no error logs within window; no downstream/upstream failure signals are observed to identify a concrete root cause. Without observable anomalies in logs or a clearly identifiable upstream failure, the data cannot robustly determine a deeper root cause. Frontend remains plausible as a localized fault or a silver-spoon symptom of an otherwise undetected upstream issue.
root_cause_candidates.reasoning: All sources fail to provide a definitive upstream/root cause. Log Agent finds no error evidence; Topology allows propagation from any dependency to frontend but does not single out frontend as a root cause; TCB-RCA places frontend as a candidate with zero anomalies detected. Given absence of observable upstream faults and no clear propagation path emerging from the data, frontend is treated as the only identifiable service directly involved, but with weak evidence of it being the root cause.

metadata.llm_reasoning: Frontend directly depends on the listed services. In absence of deeper dependency edges, a fault in any dependency could propagate to frontend, causing the symptom. The blast radius currently includes only frontend, but monitoring the dependencies is prudent since any of them could be the root; the most plausible propagation paths are from any dependency to frontend. The topology indicates no downstream services of frontend are implicated, so the hypothesis that the root cause lies in one of its dependencies is structurally consistent.

result.synthesis_reasoning: All three sources indicate no concrete root-cause evidence within the observed window. The Log Agent reports no logs indicating an anomaly; the Topology Agent shows_frontend is the symptom-bearing service driven by multiple dependencies, with plausible propagation paths from any dependency but no single clearly failing upstream; the TCB-RCA engine identifies frontend as a potential node but records zero anomalous events. Given this, there is no strong convergence on a deeper root cause. The most prudent conclusion is that the incident data is insufficient to identify a reliable root cause, and the likely root cause could be an unobserved upstream issue or a transient, non-reproducible condition not captured in the logs.
